source: This Week in Startups — AI platform shift, Nvidia outlook, extinction risk
summary: Generative AI is a fundamentally new technology platform — not a distribution channel — comparable in magnitude to the steam engine and Industrial Revolution, bigger than the internet.
summary_extended: Baker argues that generative AI represents a fundamentally new technology platform — not merely a distribution channel like the internet, iPhone, or cloud computing — but a productive force analogous to the rotary steam engine invented in 1769 that triggered the Industrial Revolution. He distinguishes GenAI from all prior tech waves by asserting it creates entirely new economic output rather than simply redistributing or accelerating access to existing goods and services. Baker frames this as the most significant technological transition since the agricultural-to-industrial shift, explicitly stating it will be 'bigger than the internet' and placing it 'somewhere between the internet and the invention of fire' in terms of civilizational impact. The investment implication is a long-term structural overweight on AI-enabling infrastructure and application companies, though this particular thesis is a macro framing argument rather than a call on any specific equity.
detail: Baker distinguishes GenAI from prior tech waves (internet, iPhone, cloud) which were all distribution channels. He frames GenAI as analogous to the rotary steam engine of 1769, predicting structural societal change at the level of the agricultural-to-industrial transition. He explicitly states it will be 'bigger than the internet.'
confidence_evidence: Baker uses unambiguous superlatives, frames it as his core investment thesis, and repeats the structural comparison multiple times with historical analogies.
quote_fragment: bigger than the internet, somewhere between the internet and the invention of fire
